input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

Now I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that benchmarks these three methods, targeting both net9.0 and net10.0. I'll create a separate benchmark project that references the TextAnalyzer, multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0, and uses BenchmarkDotNet.

Actually, BenchmarkDotNet's multi-TFM support works well here. I'll create a benchmark console project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0, includes the TextAnalyzer source directly (or references it), and runs the benchmarks.

Let me create the benchmark project. I'll include the TextAnalyzer source directly to avoid modifying the original project. Or I could reference the TextAnalyzer project but it targets only net9.0. Let me think...


The cleanest approach is to create a benchmark project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0, and directly includes the TextAnalyzer source file via a link so both frameworks get benchmarked.

+  <PropertyGroup>

+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.reasoningText:
Wait, BenchmarkDotNet's multi-TFM support works differently. The Job configuration doesn't select TFMs - you need to use `--runtimes` or configure it properly. Let me fix this. With multi-targeted projects, BenchmarkDotNet uses `[SimpleJob(RuntimeMoniker.Net90)]` and `[SimpleJob(RuntimeMoniker.Net100)]` attributes, or you run with `--runtimes net9.0 net10.0`.

Actually, the cleanest way is to use the `--runtimes` CLI argument. But we need to run it from one of the TFMs. 

I'm realizing that BenchmarkDotNet might not have RuntimeMoniker support for .NET 10 in version 0.14.0, so the most straightforward approach is running with `dotnet run -c Release -f net9.0 -- --runtimes net9.0 net10.0`, which tells BenchmarkDotNet to build and execute benchmarks across both target frameworks. The `--runtimes` flag works directly with `BenchmarkSwitcher.Run(args)`, so I just need to pass the CLI arguments through. The command should be `dotnet run -c Release -f net10.0 --project Benchmarks -- --runtimes net9.0 net10.0`, which tells BenchmarkDotNet to rebuild and run benchmarks across both target frameworks.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't recognize .NET 10. Let me write a simple manual benchmark harness instead.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't support .NET 10 runtime moniker. I need a different approach. Let me use a newer version of BenchmarkDotNet that supports .NET 10, or I'll just do manual benchmarking with a custom harness.

Let me check if there's a newer BenchmarkDotNet version... Actually, BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 was the latest stable as of early 2025. For .NET 10 (which would be late 2025), there might be a newer version.

Let me check NuGet for the latest BenchmarkDotNet version.


I'll skip the version compatibility issues and build a custom benchmark harness instead that targets both frameworks, runs proper warmup cycles, and collects timing data.
